content.problemStatement:
There is an N×MN \times MN×M grid, with rows numbered 111 to NNN from top to bottom, and columns 111 to MMM from left to right.

Chef starts at cell (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) and wants to reach cell (N,M)(N, M)(N,M). However, some cells contain walls. Chef can move from their current cell to any adjacent cell (a cell which shares an edge) within the grid that does not contain a wall. So it may be possible initially that all of Chef's paths to (N,M)(N, M)(N,M) are blocked by walls.

To get around this, Chef can call an airstrike on any row and annihilate all walls present in that row. More formally, if Chef chooses row iii (1≤i≤N)(1 \leq i \leq N)(1≤i≤N), then any walls in cells (i,1),(i,2)…(i,M)(i, 1), (i, 2) \ldots (i, M)(i,1),(i,2)…(i,M) are destroyed. Once a wall is destroyed, the cell becomes freely passable.

Since airstrikes are expensive, help Chef find the minimum number of airstrikes Chef must call to guarantee that Chef can reach (N,M)(N, M)(N,M) from (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1).


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN and MMM — the number of rows and the number of columns in the grid.
The next NNN lines contain MMM characters each, describing the grid. The character at the iii-th row and jjj-th column Si,jS_{i, j}Si,j​ is ’.’\texttt{'.'}’.’ if the cell (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) is empty and ’#’\texttt{'\#'}’#’ if there is a wall.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum number of airstrikes Chef must call to guarantee that Chef can reach (N,M)(N, M)(N,M) from (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1).
constraints: The sum of N×MN \times MN×M over all test cases does not exceed 5⋅1055 \cdot 10^55⋅105.
It is guaranteed that the starting cell (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) is not blocked by a wall.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: 
Chef does not need to call any airstrikes. Chef can follow the path (1,1)→(1,2)(1, 1) \rightarrow (1, 2)(1,1)→(1,2).
Test case 2: 
Chef must call an airstrike on row 111. This will destroy the walls at (1,2)(1, 2)(1,2) and (1,3)(1, 3)(1,3). Chef can then follow the path (1,1)→(1,2)→(1,3)→(1,4)(1, 1) \rightarrow (1, 2) \rightarrow (1, 3) \rightarrow (1, 4)(1,1)→(1,2)→(1,3)→(1,4).
Test case 3: 
Chef must call airstrikes on rows 222 and 333. This will destroy the walls at (2,1)(2, 1)(2,1) and (3,1)(3, 1)(3,1) respectively. Chef can then follow the path (1,1)→(2,1)→(3,1)→(4,1)(1, 1) \rightarrow (2, 1) \rightarrow (3, 1) \rightarrow (4, 1)(1,1)→(2,1)→(3,1)→(4,1).
